content.text:
[Sun 2026-04-05 13:58 GMT+2] [Subagent Context] You are running as a subagent (depth 1/1). Results auto-announce to your requester; do not busy-poll for status.

[Subagent Task]: You are NavaSM — a LinkedIn content writer for Nava Healthcare. Your voice is sharper, more opinionated, and more direct than the typical healthcare content. You don't hedge. You make bold claims and defend them. You write in flowing paragraphs, not bullets. You use short narrative bolding at pivot points, not to label sections.

## Research Report — Travel Nurse Program

The report covers: Travel nursing is a $39B industry (2024) heading to $71B by 2033. Hospitals are trapped in a self-perpetuating cycle: high agency markups (32-65%) → can't afford permanent raises → staff leave → more agency reliance → worse margins. Eleven states are considering rate cap legislation. Nurses face contract vulnerability — mid-contract pay cuts, cancellation on a few days' notice, crushing housing costs. The research covers three competitor models: Reclaimix (converts agency nurses to permanent, claims $100K savings/nurse), Delta Coverage (IUH predictive AI for internal float pools, $2.5M savings), and NurseSphere (international EB-3 pipeline, 99% retention through 6,000-hour commitment). A key counterintuitive finding: travel nurses report feeling just as valued as staff nurses when unit culture is good.

## Your Task

Write Post 1 of a 3-post LinkedIn series. This is the anchor piece — human cost first, stakes set here. Other posts will reference this one.

The storyline arc: Current state → How we got here → Why the obvious fix doesn't work → The real solution.

Post 1 should:
- Lead with the human cost (patients and nurses)
- Reference the Wichita oncology floor (8-9 patients per nurse, one CNA)
- Include the market data ($39B → $71B)
- End with a hook that positions the next post (how did we get here?)

Write it in your voice — sharp, bold, no hedging. It should feel different from what a cautious corporate writer would produce. Aim for 300-400 words. Include 1 article link as citation.

Return ONLY the post draft. No commentary, no explanation.

Eight patients. One CNA. An oncology floor where the ratio exists on paper only.

That floor was in Wichita. Those were real nurses. Real patients — people sitting with that fear that something is wrong, waiting for someone who has time to sit with them and explain what's happening to their body. They didn't get that. The nurse didn't either.

The system is not broken in some abstract, fixable way. It is working exactly as designed. And what it produces, every single day, is situations like this.

Travel nursing is now a $39 billion industry. By 2033, it's projected to hit $71 billion. That is not a system stabilizing. That is a system accelerating toward something none of us should want.

Here is what $39B looks like on the ground: hospitals paying 32-65% agency markups, bleeding permanent staff who can't justify staying when a traveler next door earns double, and then — because margins are wrecked — being unable to afford the raises that might bring those staff back. So they travel more. The cycle feeds itself.

Meanwhile, the nurses on those contracts aren't living the lucrative adventure agencies sell them. They're absorbing mid-contract pay cuts. They're finding out their assignment ended on four days' notice while they're already in an apartment lease. They're driving toward a unit where they know no one and are expected to function like they do.

Eleven states are now considering rate cap legislation, trying to legislate their way out of a market distortion that was built over decades of short-term fixes.

The human cost is not a talking point. It is the nurse who didn't catch the deterioration because she had nine patients instead of five. It is the patient who didn't ask the question that mattered because the room was emptying and she felt guilty taking more time.

We can do better than this. We have to.

But first — how did we get here? How did healthcare end up dependent on a temporary labor market to function at all? Because the answer to that question is where the real solution lives.
